items.title: Capsule Rocks！
items.content:
BOX GROUP, QINGDAO, CHINA — Aerogels are among the world's best thermal insulators, but they have a fatal flaw: their rigid, porous structure makes them notoriously difficult to stick to surfaces. Now, researchers at Qingdao University of Science & Technology have solved this by finding inspiration in high-tech running shoes. High-rebound sneaker midsoles use a "core-shell" bead design, where a resilient core provides bounce and a meltable outer shell acts as glue. Mimicking this, the team invented Self-Assembled Adhesive Aerogel Capsules (SAACs). These tiny capsules feature an ultralight, heat-blocking aerogel core wrapped in a heat-activated, sticky rubber shell. Using just a standard heat gun (under 80°C), the capsules can be easily molded and glued onto complex 3D surfaces. Despite being light enough to rest on a dandelion seed, they offer extreme fire protection. In tests, a thin layer of SAACs protected a model house from a direct blowtorch flame for over three minutes, keeping the inside at a safe 30°C while an unprotected house burned in seconds. This breakthrough finally paves the way for easily applied, fireproof insulation in real-world buildings.

The full research is published in Advanced Materials (DOI: 10.1002/adma.202520582).
